original_abstract: A central challenge in the emerging field of molecular spintronics is the identification of spin qubit materials capable of operating under practical conditions for quantum information applications. Recent studies have highlighted photoexcited chromophore–radical systems as promising candidates for expanding the current library of molecular qubits. In this work, we use transient electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) techniques to investigate a series of three chromophore–radical compounds consisting of a perylene diimide (PDI) chromophore linked to a stable 2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidin-1-oxyl (TEMPO) radical. By exploring the effects of isotope labeling on the TEMPO moiety, we analyze changes in the EPR spectra and electron spin coherence times ($T_m$) of the molecular quartet states generated upon photoexcitation. Our findings demonstrate that embedding these molecules in a rigid polymer matrix enables the coherent manipulation of spin states even at room temperature, reaching a spin coherence time of 0.7 $\mu$s. This observation of ambient-temperature electron spin coherence marks a significant step toward integrating functional molecular spin systems into practical quantum devices.
humanized_abstract: Spintronics holds tremendous promise for future technologies such as quantum computing and memory. One major goal for the development of molecular materials for these applications is the synthesis of materials that can act as spin qubits at ambient conditions. Potential materials have been proposed using photoexcited chromophore–radical pairs, yet none have yet been extensively characterized. In the present study, we use transient electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) to examine photoexcited species formed in three different chromophore–radical pairs consisting of perylene diimide (PDI) as the chromophore paired with 2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidin-1-oxyl (TEMPO) as the radical. Investigations of TEMPO isotopic labeling on the pairs lead to a quantitative determination of their molecular quartet compositions in their photoexcited states and to analyses of the electron spin coherence times, T m. Using these pairs in a rigid polymer matrix at ambient temperature enabled the attainment of the first demonstration of a modifiable spin material at room temperature with a value of 0.7 μs.
